title: proxy: Support convenient reads via additional image store
body:
In [this comment](https://github.com/containers/skopeo/issues/2563#issuecomment-2773572752) mtrmac said:

> That information is lost over all the abstraction layers: c/storage does not have a concept of read-only operation.

But I was thinking about this for other reasons and realized that's not strictly true; additional image stores can be used for this. It's just not very erognomic at all - one needs to allocate a temporary storage root that gets thrown away and have it use the real storage as an AIS.

For example something like this contortion for operating on my user storage:

```
$ skopeo inspect containers-storage:[overlay@/tmp/storage-root+/tmp/storage-root/run:additionalimagestore=/home/walters/.local/share/containers/storage/]localhost/bootc
```

I could imagine wrapping this whole "temporary storage root with AIS for default lower storage" in a convenient way probably in c/storage as an API and then expose it via something like `containers-storage:[overlay@:readonly]` or something as a transport?

That'd fix a major downside of this in that right now the calling tooling needs to handle discovery of the default storage path. I guess we have 
```
$ podman system info | from yaml | get store.graphRoot
/var/home/walters/.local/share/containers/storage
```
at least for that, though for consistency it'd probably be good to have this in the proxy too.

comments.body:
That’s closer to a c/storage RFE… two ways I read this:

- “keep the existing code as is, create a throwaway RW store, and the store I care about as an additional”
   - This would permit a lot of operations where the outcome would be (at best) discarded. That’s not elegant, although, I suppose, users’ responsibility.
   - We’d need to worry about cleaning up the RW store after a crash / power loss.
- “Give c/storage a native concept of a read-only store”. Cleaner but a major change of a design assumption.

(In general, the c/storage multi-store implementation already has _a lot_ of complexity. I think most of that complexity is “accidental”, but it slows us down all the same.)

---

There’s also the reliability / error recovery angle: even for read _operations_, if the primary store is inconsistent (e.g. a process is aborted in the middle of a layer creation / deletion, or some should-never-happen naming conflicts), c/storage locks the store for writing to clean things up.

In a pure-read-only operation, including as an additional image store, accessing an inconsistent store fails instead of repairing it. So, if `bootc` wants fully automatic crash recovery to happen in c/storage, read-write access is necessary.

> We’d need to worry about cleaning up the RW store after a crash / power loss.

No the proposal here is it's in a tempdir, we're just faking out c/storage essentially. There should be no writes to the "upper" storage.

> There’s also the reliability / error recovery angle: even for read operations, if the primary store is inconsistent (e.g. a process is aborted in the middle of a layer creation / deletion, or some should-never-happen naming conflicts), c/storage locks the store for writing to clean things up.

In this we get whatever the current AIS semantics are, but ideally again the process truly has read-only access (this is how we set up the LBI store with bootc, but actually also I intend to do things with other skopeo operations).

Hopefully the semantics are that any incomplete images in an AIS are ignored for read operations, can can be (as you say) cleaned up by further writes (usually, attempts to pull the image again).
Right now, store inconsistencies in AIS cause read-side-unrecoverable errors. (“The AIS has some mechanism to update it; use that mechanism to trigger a write = recovery”).
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
